topics_flat: Algebra > Algebraic Expressions > Functional Equations > Injectivity / surjectivity
Algebra > Algebraic Expressions > Functional Equations > Existential quantifiers
response_text:
No, such a function does not exist.

Brief proof:

Let \(f:(0,1)\to(2018,\infty)\) satisfy
\[
f(xy)=f(x)f(y)\qquad \forall x,y\in(0,1).
\]

Since \(f(x)>2018\) for every \(x\in(0,1)\), we have in particular \(f(x)>1\).

Now take any \(x\in(0,1)\). Then \(x^n\in(0,1)\) for every \(n\in\mathbb N\), and
\[
f(x^n)=f(x)^n.
\]
But also \(x^n\to 0\) as \(n\to\infty\), so \(f(x^n)\) must remain in \((2018,\infty)\), hence finite.

However, since \(f(x)>2018>1\), we get
\[
f(x)^n \to \infty.
\]
This means the values \(f(x^n)\) become arbitrarily large.

Now use surjectivity onto \((2018,\infty)\): for every \(y>2018\), there exists \(x\in(0,1)\) with \(f(x)=y\). In particular, choose \(y\) so large that \(y>f(t)\) for some fixed \(t\in(0,1)\). Then
\[
f(tx)=f(t)f(x)=f(t)y.
\]
Since \(f(t)>2018>1\), the value \(f(tx)\) is even larger than \(y\). Repeating this with the same \(t\), we get an unbounded multiplicative scaling of the whole image.

But the image is assumed to be exactly \((2018,\infty)\), so it must contain numbers arbitrarily close to \(2018\). This is impossible because every value in the image is multiplied by the fixed factor \(f(t)>2018\), pushing it far above \(2018\) again.

A cleaner contradiction is obtained by taking any \(x\in(0,1)\). Since \(x^n\in(0,1)\) for all \(n\),
\[
f(x^n)=f(x)^n.
\]
If \(f(x)>2018\), then \(f(x)^n\) eventually exceeds every real number. But \(x^n\) is still in the domain, so this would force the range to omit all sufficiently small numbers above \(2018\), contradicting surjectivity onto all of \((2018,\infty)\).

Therefore no such function exists.

Final answer: No, such a function does not exist.

grade.predicted: No, such a function does not exist.
